data.title: A 43-year-old billionaire has passed away; OnlyFans, which he effectively controlled, had explored the world of crypto.
data.description:
Author: Michael Mach Source: X, @1912212eth

OnlyFans once purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH.

On March 23, Leonid Radvinsky, the actual controller of OnlyFans, passed away at the age of 43 after a long battle with cancer. A spokesperson for OnlyFans only said "passed away peacefully" and requested that the public respect the privacy of his family.

OnlyFans is a content subscription platform headquartered in London, UK. Creators upload exclusive content, and fans pay monthly subscriptions. It focuses on adult content creators but also covers fitness, music, cooking, and other fields. According to the latest data in 2025, OnlyFans user accounts have stabilized at approximately 377.5 million, and the number of creators has grown to 4.63 million.

Leonid Radvinsky

Little is known about Radvinsky's stance on crypto—he hasn't publicly endorsed any blockchain, but he has explored the crypto world.

Leonid Radvinsky

The outside world knows very little about Radvinsky's attitude towards crypto—he hasn't publicly endorsed any blockchain, but he has explored the crypto world.

Having purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH and donated to the Ukrainian DAO, OnlyFans quietly launched a small feature in February 2022 during his tenure: supporting Ethereum-verified NFTs as avatars. The platform explicitly stated that this was "the first step in exploring the role of NFTs on the platform." Creators' NFT avatars would have a small Ethereum icon, which could be clicked to jump to OpenSea for details. This seemingly insignificant feature allowed millions of adult creators to directly access crypto assets for the first time. This move was early. That year, the NFT craze was at its peak; Twitter launched NFT avatars, and OnlyFans quickly followed suit. What is little known is that OnlyFans' parent company, Fenix ​​International, also purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH. According to the company's financial statements as of the end of November 2022, Fenix ​​purchased a total of approximately $19.9 million worth of ETH, classifying it as intangible assets. Due to the cryptocurrency market crash in 2022, the price of Ethereum plummeted, and the company recorded an impairment loss of approximately $8.46 million that year, adjusting the value of its ETH holdings to approximately $11.4 million. The OnlyFans platform still does not support crypto payments; users rely on credit cards or third-party virtual cards. During Radvinsky's era, OnlyFans was more like a hybrid of "traditional finance + adult content," taking a 20% cut and maintaining an incredibly stable cash flow. Turning our attention to the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, a group of cryptocurrency activists and enthusiasts quickly rallied to support Ukraine. Their method was to auction off an NFT artwork depicting the Ukrainian flag. An auction held by the decentralized autonomous organization "Ukraine DAO" raised 2,258 Ether, worth approximately $6.79 million at the time. According to a subsequent report by Decrypt, the adult website OnlyFans also participated in the donation. Blockchain tracking platform Etherscan shows that on February 27th, an address named only.eth donated 500 ETH to the Ukrainian DAO, now worth $1.079 million.

Etherscan transfer screenshot

Zapper data shows that the only.eth address was created in May 2021, and its current wallet value is less than $2,000. The last wallet transfer activity was 3 years ago.

However, it was its founder, Tim Stokely, who truly brought the OnlyFans DNA into cryptography.

Tim Stokely

In 2016, Stokely founded the platform OnlyFans. In December 2021, he resigned as CEO, making a complete career change. In May 2022, he and former OnlyFans executive RJ Phillips launched Zoop—an NFT trading card platform based on the Polygon blockchain.

... Zoop is positioned as completely "family-friendly": selling 3D digital collectible cards featuring celebrities and influencers, which users can buy, sell, trade, and collect, and even receive airdrop rewards. It features limited releases and emphasizes revenue sharing for creators. Essentially, Zoop brings OnlyFans' "direct payment to fans" model to the Web3 collectibles market. In April 2025, Zoop, in conjunction with the HBAR Foundation (the treasury manager of the Hedera blockchain), submitted a bid to acquire TikTok's US operations. The core of the proposal is Web3 integration: Hedera's scalability will be used to support TikTok's NFTs, creator payments, governance structure, and overall token incentive system, with the goal of directly distributing 80% of advertising revenue to creators and users. Ultimately, for various reasons, the intended bid did not go through. Tim Stokely, having withdrawn from the adult entertainment industry, brought his core logic to the TikTok bidding for Zoop and Hedera, essentially upgrading the "fans pay for content" model to a Web3 version.

data.title: How to build the strongest Ethereum? Reshaping the relationship between L1 and L2
data.description:
Authors: Josh Rudolf, Julian Ma, Josh Stark Source: Ethereum Foundation Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

Goal: All users (individuals and institutions) should have a clear path to leverage, extend, and benefit from Ethereum's core features.

The optimal path to achieving this goal is to fully leverage the unique capabilities of each layer, solidify Ethereum's core attributes, and create tangible value for end users through these attributes.

As the ecosystem develops, the roles of each layer are constantly evolving:

  • In the past: L2's primary goal was to scale Ethereum, followed by leaving room for differentiation and customization; the core focus was on scaling.

    • In the past: L2's primary goal was to scale Ethereum, followed by leaving room for differentiation and customization; the core focus was on scaling.

      • Today: The primary goal of L2 is to provide differentiated features, services, customization options, market strategies, and controllable domains, while also offering additional scaling capabilities. The core focus has shifted to differentiation, control, and innovation. L1's positioning: As a truly permissionless and highly resilient global hub, it undertakes the core functions of settlement, shared state, liquidity, and DeFi. A robust L1 that is uncompromising on CROPS (censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security) and continuously scaling provides a more solid foundation for L2. L2's positioning: To provide valuable new features, customization options, and control, developing its own on-chain economy while extending Ethereum's core features to more users. A strong L2 network will feed back into the ecosystem, consolidating Ethereum's central position. L2 platforms come in various forms, and their degree of integration with L1 can be flexibly adjusted according to their own needs. Integration with L1: L2 platforms aiming for deep integration with L1 should move towards mechanisms such as synchronous composability, full interoperability, shared liquidity, Stage 2, and native Rollups. Coverage: Various L2 platforms with diverse business models and technical expertise will continue to play an important role in the ecosystem, providing capabilities that L1 lacks. The Ethereum Foundation (EF) will continue to invest in technological research and development to enable L2 platforms to more seamlessly extend the core attributes of L1 and securely access liquidity and funds across L1 and other L2 platforms. L2 platforms should maintain the transparency and verifiability of their security attributes. In short, both sides bear important missions, and their ideals and practices should be consistent.

        Introduction

        Over the past five years, a number of blockchain ecosystems have grown around Ethereum L1. These chains can choose to inherit different characteristics of Ethereum: some fully inherit decentralization (such as Stage 2 Rollups), some inherit some security attributes (such as Validium, Prividium), and others are built solely on the general EVM standard (not L2). Many projects are still under development, often starting as independent chains and gradually integrating deeply with Ethereum L1.

        Now, the Ethereum Foundation and the broader Ethereum ecosystem need to update our understanding of the relationship between L1 and L2 networks. The last systematic review was about five years ago, when a roadmap centered on Rollups was first proposed as the direction for Ethereum scaling.

        Since then, the industry has undergone tremendous changes: the technology supporting L2 sharing Ethereum security and liquidity and achieving interoperability has matured and evolved; the competitive advantages and user value of L2 have become increasingly clear; L2 itself has grown and developed into an independent ecosystem and community; and the L1 scaling roadmap has become clearer and more defined. As an ecosystem community, we need to face these changes squarely and learn from the successes and failures of the ecosystem. In recent months, the future relationship between Ethereum L1 and L2 has become increasingly clear: A thriving Ethereum ecosystem must be rooted in a strong L1 foundation. Ethereum L1 will achieve orders-of-magnitude scaling while retaining the highest level of security and decentralization, continuing to serve as the heart of the on-chain economy and a DeFi hub. The independent and interoperable L2 ecosystem will continue to expand, providing a high degree of customization, control, and functionality that L1 cannot achieve. These L2s choose to be rooted in the Ethereum ecosystem because it is the optimal solution for their users, community, and businesses. L2s both compete and cooperate (which is healthy and positive), providing diverse and specialized block spaces, services, and assets. This article aims to elaborate on the development vision of L1 and L2 and build a mutually empowering cooperative path for Ethereum L1 and all chains that wish to integrate into the ecosystem. What are the positioning of L1 and L2, and how do they collaborate? Ethereum L1 is the world's leading programmable blockchain. In terms of adoption rate, developer attention, decentralization, resilience, and robustness, no other chain can currently rival it. Ethereum L1 is the core of the DeFi ecosystem, possessing the deepest liquidity. Currently, Ethereum L1 has a clear scaling path while adhering to decentralization and robustness. Thanks to the efforts of numerous teams within the ecosystem, the development speed of zero-knowledge (ZK) technology has exceeded expectations. In the coming years, Ethereum L1 can achieve scaling by several orders of magnitude without compromising its core values ​​and vision. However, at the same time, no single chain can meet the diverse needs of a globalized on-chain economy. Even if Ethereum remains the world's leading blockchain and achieves a thousandfold increase in scaling, the market will still need a large number of different chains—because they can provide specialization and customization capabilities that no L1 blockchain can achieve: Specialized design for specific applications and scenarios; Non-EVM functionality; Stronger privacy protection; Pricing mechanisms or transaction packaging logic; Ultra-low latency and other sorting features; Extreme scaling capabilities unattainable by L1 blockchains; Specialized economic models, market strategies, and growth methods. Modular design to support compliance or other business needs. Other improvements and innovations that can be iterated and implemented faster beyond L1. Governance strategies that give stakeholders fine-grained control over their flexible execution environment on Ethereum. This creates an opportunity for Ethereum L1 and L2 to establish a mutually beneficial relationship, with each fulfilling its role and complementing each other's strengths. Why should other chains become L2 on Ethereum? High security, low adversary risk, high decentralization, and significantly reduced costs. L2 can achieve the highest security and decentralization at a cost far lower than replacing L1. Building and incentivizing a global decentralized validator integration is costly, time-consuming, and extremely difficult. L2 can transfer this responsibility to Ethereum L1, paying based on usage, rather than bearing the huge fixed costs of building its own L1 validator network. Users and developers can reach more users and developers by interoperating with the largest L1 and largest L2 networks (ZK technology, real-time proofs, faster L1 finality and L2 settlement, and mature smart proxy infrastructure will further accelerate interoperability and cross-chain experiences). A well-designed interoperability L2 can securely access L1 assets, DeFi liquidity, L1 user accounts, and L1 services such as oracles and ENS. Marketing benefits include the brand and reputation advantages of being part of the Ethereum ecosystem—Ethereum boasts the strongest reputation, security record, and regulatory approval among all L1 platforms. How can Ethereum L1 benefit from this relationship? Based on experience and communication with various stakeholders in the ecosystem, we believe that placing Ethereum L1 at the center of the ever-expanding L2 network will solidify Ethereum and ETH's unique position in the on-chain economy: Creating demand for ETH and enabling trust-free, secure cross-chain bridging between ETH and other assets. ETH simultaneously functions as a store of value, currency, and application platform within the Ethereum ecosystem. Amplifying Ethereum's network effects (such as EVM, developer education, toolchains, user onboarding, and L2 interoperability). Consolidating Ethereum's value as the core of the multi-chain ecosystem and the primary settlement and liquidity layer for the on-chain economy. L2 will help Ethereum achieve its core vision: to serve as a distribution engine for Ethereum's core attributes (security, resilience, robustness) (providing additional scaling), allowing more people to sustainably benefit from Ethereum. The Ethereum ecosystem should not take these benefits for granted. Some viewpoints remain controversial within the community, and some long-standing arguments still need to be verified through experimentation, measurement, and analysis. Ultimately, the relationship between L1 and L2 must be mutually beneficial to succeed. The first five years of this relationship have yielded many achievements and laid a key foundation for the future. What does this mean for the future development of L2? What does this new vision mean for L2, its team, and the community? Our recommendations are as follows: L2 platforms should focus on strategies that complement L1 platforms and differentiate themselves. Many L2 platforms have successfully implemented this approach: building unique communities and extending Ethereum features to millions of new users through innovative functionalities, vertical scenarios (such as application chains), new distribution models, or novel marketing strategies. L2 platforms should boldly explore all feasible directions for differentiation. Emerging directions include scalability, trustlessness, privacy, corporate compliance, industry verticals, community operations, and various technological innovations. Other suitable scenarios for L2 platforms include encrypted electronic voting bulletin boards and certificate transparency systems. L2 platforms can choose to fully or partially inherit Ethereum attributes based on their own goals, which is a reasonable choice, but users must clearly understand the security attributes they provide and do not provide.

      • A trustless L2 should at least reach Stage 1 and pass the withdrawal test: even if there are malicious operators or the security committee fails, users can still safely withdraw to L1. Choose the L2 that is closest to L1 and fully inherits its attributes. The following directions should be taken: Implement Stage 2. Achieve synchronous composability between the protocol layer and the application layer (including read-only operations like L1SLOAD/L1STATICCALL and read-write operations). Become a native Rollup (removing the security committee). L2 should continuously promote interoperability and shared liquidity mechanisms to strengthen the entire system. We encourage teams to research the Open Intents Framework and Fast Confirmation Rule, explore designs that allow L1 funds to be accessed without leaving L2, and participate in work related to synchronous composability. L2 should operate transparently, clearly explaining its security attributes and relevance to L1 to the ecosystem (L2Beat plays a crucial role in improving L2 ecosystem transparency and driving continuous improvement). The Ethereum Foundation's Work in This Area: To realize this vision of the relationship between L1 and L2, the Ethereum Foundation has clarified its mission and is advancing the following work: Promoting the scaling of L1 and Blob data without sacrificing decentralization and robustness. Currently, Blob utilization is only about 30%, leaving ample room for growth and the potential for significant scaling if necessary. **Focus on supporting L2 platforms that already possess or wish to enhance capabilities in core areas such as privacy, security, and trustlessness.** **Led by Josh Rudolf, the platform team is optimizing the Ethereum platform as the interface between L2 and the core protocol roadmap.** **Improve L1 liquidity and simplify the process for L2 platforms to access liquidity (faster finality, withdrawals, and deposits).** **Work closely with L2 teams to understand their needs and prioritize them in the protocol, clarifying the relationship between L1 and L2. We need to identify what works and what doesn't, collaborate, and always clearly define and reinforce the value of being part of the Ethereum ecosystem.** **Invest in R&D to support native Rollup technology—L2 platforms that can be fully trustlessly verified by L1, enabling simultaneous composability and secure interoperability.** Working closely with organizations like L2Beat, we will monitor and validate L2 security attributes. We must rigorously and honestly disclose L2 attributes and the extent to which they share L1 security, enabling users and developers to make informed choices. Addressing a major drawback of multi-chain ecosystems: fragmentation. We will collaborate with ecosystem partners (chains, wallets, infrastructure providers) to create better interoperability solutions, improving user experience and alleviating fragmentation on developer platforms. As the vision for the relationship between L1 and L2 becomes clearer, we will also address the fragmentation of the Ethereum narrative.

data.title: Why did Tether suddenly undergo a full audit? Is the stablecoin era entering a transparent phase?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

On March 24, 2026, Tether announced that it had officially commissioned one of the Big Four accounting firms (Deloitte, EY, KPMG, and PwC) to conduct its first comprehensive independent financial statement audit. This audit is expected to be the largest first audit in the history of the financial market. Its scale is extremely rare outside of sovereign institutions, and it covers an extremely complex combination of digital assets, traditional reserves, and tokenized liabilities.

Tether CFO Simon McWilliams stated, "We selected this Big Four accounting firm through a competitive process; the audit will be completed smoothly." As of press time, Tether has not disclosed which accounting firm is conducting the audit.

Why is Tether conducting such a large-scale audit? What will be the impact?

I. Responding to the "Trust Crisis"

Previously, the market had a long-standing trust crisis regarding Tether—a lack of confidence in the authenticity of its reserves.

Last December, BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes warned that Tether could face serious problems if the value of USDT's reserve assets fell. In 2017, Tether pledged to conduct regular audits of its assets; however, in January 2018, the company terminated its partnership with its auditing firm (Friedman LLP) without completing the audit, exacerbating concerns about the source of USDT's funds. Around the same time, the closely related Bitfinex exchange experienced banking difficulties, unable to access $850 million in customer funds entrusted to payment processors. In 2019, the New York State Attorney General's Office (NYAG) launched an investigation into Tether and Bitfinex, accusing Bitfinex of jeopardizing USDT's funding by misappropriating Tether's dollar reserves to cover funding shortfalls. The dispute was settled in February 2021, with Tether's parent company, iFinex, agreeing to pay an $18.5 million fine to the New York State Attorney General's Office and cease trading with New York customers; however, neither company admitted to wrongdoing. Tether's transparency and reserve practices have been a focus of regulatory scrutiny. In October 2021, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) fined Tether $41 million for making misleading statements about its reserves. The CFTC found that during certain periods between 2016 and 2018, Tether's fiat currency reserves were less than the circulating supply of USDT, contradicting its past public claims that each token had adequate reserves. Tether described the scope of this audit as "extremely comprehensive." The accounting firm responsible for the audit stated that the level of this audit "is typically only achieved by the world's largest national institutions." This audit will carefully examine Tether's complex asset portfolio, which primarily comprises three core components: Digital Assets: This category includes cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Ethereum held in its reserves. Traditional Reserves: These are traditional assets such as U.S. Treasury bills, commercial paper, and cash equivalents. Tokenized Government Bonds: This represents a newer, innovative asset class involving blockchain-based sovereign debt. Tether has a long history of trust crises, and this more comprehensive audit is expected to address these concerns. II. Regulatory Needs Stablecoins have evolved from crypto-transaction tools into a part of global payments and financial stability. The EU's MiCA Act, the US draft stablecoin bill, and various countries' regulatory requirements regarding anti-money laundering and the source of funds are constantly tightening. Transparent reserves, auditability, and near-bank regulation are the current consensus in the stablecoin industry. According to Cryptorank, Tether previously commissioned a smaller accounting firm, BDO Italia, to issue an assurance report, which only provided an overview of its assets. However, these assurance reports are fundamentally different from a full audit. Audits follow strict international standards and issue formal opinions on the fairness of financial statements. Assurance reports, on the other hand, only verify information at a specific point in time. Throughout 2024, regulators intensified their push for higher verification standards. Specifically, regulatory frameworks such as the EU's Crypto Asset Markets (MiCA) regulations and proposed US legislation began to mandate that stablecoin issuers comply with stringent reserve reporting and auditing requirements. Therefore, Tether's proactive measures appear strategically aligned with this global regulatory trend, demonstrating its preemptive approach to compliance requirements and establishing legitimacy. This move signifies a step towards greater financial transparency for Tether.

III. Responding to Competition from Circle

Tether remains the leading stablecoin in the cryptocurrency industry, with a market capitalization of approximately $184 billion, more than double that of Circle's USDC (which has a market capitalization of $78 billion). However, a report released earlier this month by Japanese investment bank Mizuho Bank stated that Circle's stablecoin trading volume has surpassed USDT's for the first time since 2019: USDC trading volume is approximately $2.2 trillion, while USDT trading volume is $1.3 trillion.

III. Responding to Competition from Circle

Tether remains the leading stablecoin in the cryptocurrency industry, with a market capitalization of approximately $184 billion, more than double that of Circle's USDC (which has a market capitalization of $78 billion).

Mizuho Bank stated, "Data shows that USDC accounts for 64% of trading volume relative to USDT. This reverses the long-term trend from 2019 to 2025 where USDT trading volume exceeded USDC." Back in June 2022, Circle CFO Jeremy Fox-Geen wrote an article titled "How to Build Trust," which explicitly mentioned that since its launch in 2018, the USDC reserve has been audited annually by a leading global accounting firm as part of Circle's annual financial statements. This audit aims to verify the accuracy, completeness, and composition of the reserve, and to test internal controls that ensure the accuracy of the financial statements. In addition to annual audits, Circle has published monthly reports on the size and composition of USDC reserves since its launch in 2018 to ensure that the reserve size is at least equivalent to the circulating supply of USDC. Circle's proactive cooperation with regulators, greater asset transparency, and closer alignment with the traditional banking system clearly make it easier to gain favor than Tether. The stablecoin market previously relied on liquidity and market share, but now transparency and compliance are paramount. If Tether does not conduct compliance audits, it will find itself in a passive position. Therefore, following Circle's lead in the stablecoin market, Tether has also had to embark on the path of compliance—to counter competition from compliant stablecoins like USDC and solidify its leading position through audit endorsements. IV. Institutional Entry and the Need for Transparency
Heavy participants in the crypto market have shifted from retail investors to institutions. Institutional funds are subject to strict constraints and rely more on audited financial information for decision-making; they must hold legal assets within a regulatory framework; if USDT is non-compliant, it cannot be held by institutions as an investable asset. This audit will allow USDT to be fully held by institutions. Furthermore, due to USDT's enormous size, as the largest source of liquidity in the crypto market, a foundational asset of DeFi, and a cross-border payment tool, even the slightest mistake by Tether could have a huge impact on the crypto and traditional financial markets. Market observers believe that any major misstep by the company could trigger a "black swan" collapse for the entire industry. During the deleveraging wave of 2022 and the FTX bankruptcy, there were particular concerns about such issues, but USDT ultimately maintained its pegged price and market position. Tether has become so large that transparency is essential. The most direct impact is for Tether to prove its innocence. The timing of this audit is crucial: regulators and market participants are demanding stronger proof of reserves from stablecoin issuers. A comprehensive audit is expected to dispel long-standing industry concerns about whether USDT has sufficient reserves and liquidity. Dr. Anya Petrova, a fintech researcher at the Global Institute for Digital Finance, points out, "For Tether, this is less about demonstrating solvency (although its audit report has aimed to do so), and more about adopting the rigorous and repeatable disclosure framework required by traditional capital markets. This could significantly reduce the risk premium perceived by institutions interacting with the USDT ecosystem." Secondly, it will set an example for compliance standards in the stablecoin industry. Circle and others will face greater competitive pressure and may also undergo similar audits, thereby raising the overall compliance level of the sector. Thirdly, a compliant Tether will further attract institutional attention. After the audit, traditional financial institutions can overcome the compliance hurdles of USDT, further increasing USDT adoption and consolidating Tether's leading position in the crypto space. Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino stated, “Tether’s mission has always been to build trust through actions, not promises. Trust is built on an institution’s willingness to undergo comprehensive scrutiny. This audit represents years of effort to strengthen the system, aiming to bring Tether to the highest standards in the global financial sector. For the hundreds of millions of individuals and businesses that rely on the US dollar₮ every day, this audit is more than just a compliance review; it’s about accountability, resilience, and confidence in the infrastructure they rely on.” “By completing this comprehensive audit, Tether aims to further strengthen the global foundation of trust underpinning the USD₮ and support the company’s mission to expand the freedom people have to store, send, and use their funds.” This audit is not only a turning point for Tether itself but may also herald a new phase for the stablecoin industry: “audit and regulation driven.”

data.title: OpenAI puts the car in reverse; 1-year-old Sora is euthanized.
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing, Tencent Technology

On March 24, 2026, OpenAI announced the shutdown of Sora.

This was OpenAI's first standalone application after ChatGPT, and it had only been officially launched for six months. On the same day, Disney announced the termination of its $1 billion investment and character licensing agreement with OpenAI, as the two parties had never completed the financial transaction.

According to foreign media reports, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told employees at an internal meeting that shutting down Sora was to "free up resources for the next generation of AI models." Altman also announced that he would shift his personal focus to fundraising and building computing infrastructure. OpenAI's new model, codenamed "Spud," has completed initial development.

Sora was not a failed product.

Sora was not a failed product.

... Launched in September 2025, Sora surpassed one million downloads within ten days, briefly topping the App Store's free charts—a speed that even surpassed ChatGPT. However, according to mobile analytics firm Appfigures, Sora's downloads peaked at approximately 3.33 million in November 2025 before rapidly declining, dropping to around 1.13 million by February 2026. Throughout its entire lifecycle, in-app purchase revenue amounted to only about $2.1 million. Shutting down Sora itself isn't the most important thing. The important thing is that it marks OpenAI's formal admission that its "do everything" strategy of the past year has failed. And this admission of error didn't come early.

Too many "copycat tasks," the main storyline is lost

2025 was the year of the most expansion for OpenAI's product line. Video generation model Sora, AI browser Atlas, hardware devices developed in collaboration with Jony Ive, e-commerce functionality in ChatGPT—Altman once likened this strategy to "betting on a series of startups within OpenAI."

This logic was reasonable at the time.

With ChatGPT boasting 900 million weekly active users, OpenAI seems poised to launch attacks in multiple directions simultaneously, solidifying its position as a "definitive AI era" leader. However, the internal reality is quite different. Several current and former employees report that **too many parallel projects have led to a blurred strategic direction, with computing resources frequently being reallocated between teams.** The organizational structure is also becoming increasingly chaotic; **for example, the Sora team is placed under the research department, but it is actually responsible for one of the company's most anticipated products.** On March 16, 2026, Applications CEO Fidji Simo defined a new direction at an all-hands meeting. Foreign media reported on the meeting minutes, in which Simo said, "We cannot miss this moment because we are distracted by side quests." She further stated, "We really have to nail productivity in general and particularly productivity on the business front." Simo described Anthropic's rise as a "wake-up call," and said the company was "very much acting as if it's a code red." While shutting down Sora, OpenAI's new strategy has only two keywords: code and enterprise. OpenAI is merging ChatGPT, the programming tool Codex, and the browser Atlas into a single desktop "super app." Sora's video generation capabilities will no longer exist in any independent form, and the team will shift towards long-term research in robotics and world simulation. OpenAI's strategic shift is directly attributable to Anthropic's rapid rise in the coding and enterprise markets. According to data from fintech company Ramp, as of February 2026, Anthropic accounted for approximately 73% of spending among enterprises making their first purchase of AI tools, compared to OpenAI's approximately 27%. In the overall enterprise AI subscription market, OpenAI still leads with 34.4% to 24.4%, but Anthropic's month-over-month growth rate far exceeds OpenAI's. In February 2026, Anthropic grew by 4.9%, while OpenAI declined by 1.5% in the same month—OpenAI's largest single-month drop since Ramp began tracking enterprise AI purchases. Ramp economist Ara Kharazian points out that a year ago, only one in twenty-five companies paid for Anthropic; now that figure is one in a quarter. [Image: Growth curves of the two companies since they each reached $1 billion in annualized revenue; Anthropic's annual growth is approximately 10 times vs. OpenAI's approximately 3.4 times.] The gap is even more significant in the highest-value sub-segment, Coding. According to a Menlo Ventures report, Anthropic's Claude Code holds approximately 54% of the programming market share, while OpenAI holds only about 21%. After its public release in May 2025, Claude Code reached $1 billion in annualized revenue in just six months, and by February 2026, it had exceeded $2.5 billion. 70%-90% of the code produced by Anthropic's own engineering team is generated using Claude Code, and Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, stated that 100% of his daily coding is done using this tool.

Chart: Monthly Trend of Enterprise AI Procurement Rate -- Changes in Enterprise Subscription Procurement Rates of OpenAI (approx. 34.4%) vs. Anthropic (approx. 24.4%), with OpenAI's curve trend turning downwards

This is not just a success of "programming". Anthropic released Cowork in January 2026, a desktop agent tool for non-developers that can handle spreadsheets, file management, and report generation. ...p>

Four engineers completed the development in ten days, with most of the code written by Claude Code itself. After Cowork's release, the global SaaS sector saw a loss of approximately $2 trillion in market capitalization. In contrast, Anthropic's strategy has always been focused: no audio, no images, no video, only text, code, and enterprise scenarios. It's completely different from OpenAI, betting all its limited computing power on coding, which has proven to be the area with the highest return on investment. It's not just the product; the people, the architecture, and the North Star may all be at fault. OpenAI's deeper challenges go far beyond its multi-pronged product strategy. Since 2024, OpenAI has experienced an unprecedented loss of core talent. Co-founder and Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever left OpenAI in May 2024 to found Safe Superintelligence (SSI), which was valued at over $30 billion in less than a year. Jan Leike, co-head of the Superintelligence team, resigned around the same time, publicly criticizing OpenAI's security culture as "giving way to glamorous products," and subsequently joined Anthropic. CTO Mira Murati left in September 2024 to found Thinking Machines Lab, while Chief Research Officer Bob McGrew and VP of Research Barret Zoph left on the same day. Co-founder John Schulman first went to Anthropic before joining Murati's company. According to the Seoul Economic Daily, over 50 researchers and engineers have left for competitors such as Anthropic and Meta in the past year. By early 2026, of OpenAI's original 11 co-founders, only Altman and President Greg Brockman remain with the company. These individuals were all pillars of OpenAI in certain areas. Sutskever was the driving force behind OpenAI's technical direction, leading core research breakthroughs from the GPT series to inference models (such as O1). His departure likely means the company has lost the most insightful person on the fundamental question of "which direction the model should evolve in." Murati, on the other hand, was the key figure in translating research results into products; her role was irreplaceable for the launch of ChatGPT. When a company simultaneously loses its technological leader and product hub, who can make the right decisions? Judging from recent photos, Sam Altman's hair has turned completely white. The tug-of-war between capital, IPO, and strategy: OpenAI's current situation is compounded by a unique pressure: it may become the largest unlisted company in human history, and its cash burn rate is astonishing. In February 2026, OpenAI completed an $110 billion funding round, valuing the company at $730 billion pre-money and approximately $840 billion post-money, making it the largest single-round private equity funding in history. Amazon invested $50 billion, while Nvidia and SoftBank each invested $30 billion. However, these figures come with numerous conditions: Amazon's $35 billion is contingent on OpenAI achieving its AGI target or going public. In 2025, OpenAI's revenue was approximately $13.1 billion, but it incurred a loss of approximately $8 billion during the same period. According to foreign media reports, losses are projected to soar to $25 billion in 2026, and cumulative losses could reach $115 billion from now until 2029. OpenAI expects to become profitable no earlier than the 2030s. Its cash burn rate in 2026 is estimated at approximately 83.3%, an extremely rare occurrence in startup history. This capital structure forms a delicate but fragile closed loop: Nvidia is both an investor and a GPU supplier, Amazon and Microsoft are both investors and cloud service providers, and SoftBank is a partner in the Stargate data center project. A significant portion of the funding will flow back to investors in the form of service fees. Altman acknowledged the validity of this concern in a CNBC interview, but stated that "it only makes sense if new revenue flows into the entire AI ecosystem." Under this capital structure, an IPO is an unavoidable choice. OpenAI aims to go public by the end of 2026. Before going public, it must present a clear growth story to public market investors. This is the direct driving force behind the strategic shift of "cutting redundancy and focusing on productivity." Meanwhile, Anthropic's funding is equally impressive, completing a $30 billion Series G round in February 2026 with a valuation of $38 billion. However, its revenue structure appears healthier: approximately 80% comes from enterprise clients, with annualized revenue reaching about $14 billion, maintaining roughly tenfold growth for three consecutive years. Research firm Epoch AI predicts that if the current growth trend continues, Anthropic's annual revenue could surpass OpenAI's in the second half of 2026. The giants haven't fallen yet. The competition is far from over; OpenAI still possesses significant structural advantages: over 800 million weekly active users, the highest consumer AI brand awareness globally, and a newly acquired $110 billion in funding. ChatGPT's consumer subscription revenue continues to grow, its enterprise clients have exceeded 1 million, and its new model, Spud, is about to be released. Codex programming tools already have over 2 million weekly active users. However, the AI ​​industry is at a critical juncture of paradigm shift, with the focus of value moving from "whose model is the strongest" to "who can help companies improve productivity," and from a competition of general capabilities to Agentic AI—AI systems capable of autonomously completing complex tasks. In this paradigm shift, Anthropic bet on the right direction, while OpenAI bet everything, but essentially bet nothing. The shutdown of Sora is a signal that OpenAI's management has recognized the problem. However, there is still a long way to go between recognizing the problem and solving it. With the founding team almost entirely gone, unprecedented capital obligations, and competitors already ahead, can OpenAI return to its original form, maintain its initial goals, and make the right choices? From a laboratory to the world's most valuable unlisted company, OpenAI took less than four years. The next four quarters will determine whether it can transform from a "big" company into a "right" company, or, in extreme cases, a company heading towards decline.

data.title: Some insights and reflections on Openclaw shrimp farming
data.description:
Author: Haotian; Source: X, @tmel0211

As a "shrimp farmer," after several weeks of painful struggle, I'd like to share some insights and experiences on shrimp farming for your reference only::

1) Everyone knows that shrimp farming is for "efficiency improvement," but the truth is that at this stage, most ordinary people are "wasting their time" in shrimp farming. There are many unexpected troubles, such as Claude's account being banned, API forwarding quotas being blocked, and Openclaw suddenly upgrading and losing its "memory," etc. This will consume most of the time, and the efficiency gains brought are far from proportional; 2) I saw various posts on Twitter timelines selling AI anxiety. Just take a look and move on. Such as how a single command can make Claude control your entire computer, or how a single prompt will allow AI to permanently take over your work and make you lie down, blah blah blah. The reality is, not only is it difficult to work comfortably, but people often code until the wee hours of the morning for a single feature, frequently fixing one bug only to have another pop up. It's hard to imagine how those who aren't even willing to actually operate the system for five minutes can so confidently proclaim the arrival of AGI and that AI will revolutionize everything; 3) Raising lobsters can indeed fulfill many people's OPC (One Person Company) dreams, but while the upper limit of a large model's capabilities is equal for everyone, the cognitive level of different people in managing large models varies drastically. Don't assume everyone can become Peter Steinberger, Matt Schlicht, or Andrej Karpathy. The thinking, framework design, iteration experience, ability level, and delivery results of a brilliant developer and an ordinary person in their so-called lobster-raising can be worlds apart; 4) Raising lobsters essentially builds your own personalized AI. The OS, theoretically, involves multiple parallel connections between large models, managing the number of digital employees, the levels of proactive and reactive collaboration, the friction of nested skills and combined collaborative task delivery, etc. The more overlapping requirements of Cron, jobs, real-time data scanning, and trading skills, the greater the probability of conflict and the greater the optimization challenge. It's important to understand that shrimp farming is never a "model capability" problem, but rather an "engineering implementation optimization" problem. 5) Configuring all digital employees with Opus 4.6 and Gemini 2.5 Flash lite might both accomplish the same task, but the former is like hiring seasoned Wall Street elites, while the latter is more like hiring slaves from the slums. They might both achieve the same function, but the cost and the delivered result are incomparable. The truth is, the time you spend painstakingly debugging might be easily replaced by someone else simply by having the financial means. Shrimp farming is incredibly expensive. What's frightening is that most people understand this, yet they still compromise and constantly optimize with subpar models. (6) Managing digital employees is like stacking Lego bricks: the more employees, the more skills, and the more complex the job scenarios, the higher the probability of sudden collapse.) For some tasks, just be capable enough; don't be insatiable and impose "requirements" beyond your cognitive boundaries. It's highly recommended to invest more effort in memory engineering, Git version control, and dispelling model illusions; otherwise, there will be many moments that will push you to the brink of collapse. One second you might be feeling great about completing a cool requirement, and the next second everything will have collapsed, leaving you heartbroken. Don't ask me how I know. (That's all.)

data.title: Interview with Bitwise CIO: The Mathematical Logic Behind Bitcoin Reaching $1 Million
data.description:
Note: Is Bitcoin reaching $1 million just a "matter of time" or a "pipe dream"? Bitwise Chief Investment Officer Matt Hougan offered an extremely conservative yet striking mathematical projection in a recent interview. He believes that even without considering aggressive growth, Bitcoin has the potential for a 20-fold increase based solely on the natural expansion of the store-of-value market. Matt Hougan recently stated this on Deribit's podcast, Crypto Options Unplugged.

Matt Hougan engaged in in-depth discussions on topics of concern to the crypto industry, including DAT, the long-term value of Bitcoin, the quantum threat, the relationship between AI and crypto, and the Clarity Act. Furthermore, Matt made a keen observation: the real "crypto winter" is not far off, but has already quietly arrived in the retail sector since the beginning of 2025.

When institutional funding flows mask retail investor exits, when the quantum threat is actually slowing down the decision-making of $50 billion funds, and when AI agents begin to reshape the on-chain financial landscape, where exactly are we in the cycle? Matt Hougan provides an in-depth analysis of Bitwise's blueprint for a crypto boom (2027), the brand moat of L1 space, and the foundation that determines the industry's survival—the Clarity Act. Jinse Finance has compiled and organized the main content of the podcast, as follows:

CoreKey Points：

  1. ETF vs. Digital AssetsTreasury (DAT):ETFs are strictly superior to DAT in terms of pure holding. DAT must transform into "Version 2.0"—creating additional alpha through options strategies, leverage, lending, etc., otherwise it will be eliminated.

  2. The Long-Term Value of Bitcoin:Based on the logic of "digital gold," with the continued growth of the store of value market and Bitcoin's share rising from 5% to 15%, Bitcoin is expected to reach $1 million within 10 years, a rather conservative assumption. Quantum Threat: Technically solvable, but currently slowing institutional adoption—it's a recurring question at every roadshow, extending decision-making cycles by 6 to 9 months. AI and Crypto Relationship: Long-term Complementarity: AI triggers deflation → government money printing → positive for Bitcoin; short-term increased correlation with tech stocks may simply be a statistical anomaly. Market Cycle Assessment: The crypto retail winter began in early 2025 and may be bottoming out now, with a significantly better year expected by the end of the year; 2027 is expected to be a big year for crypto. Ethereum and Solana: The core issue is whether L1 block space will be fully commoditized. Matt is bullish on both, believing that brand moats will persist. Regulatory Risks: The Clarity Act has an approximate 50/50 chance of passing. If passed, it will lay a solid foundation for the industry; if not passed, the risk of partisanism in crypto will persist. Deribit: We always ask our guests how they entered the crypto space, when they entered, and what made them make that choice. Can you tell us about it? Matt Hogan: I joined Bitwise full-time in early 2018. Before that, I was the CEO of ETF.com, having worked in the ETF industry for over ten years. After selling that company, I started looking for my next thing, and cryptocurrency was an obvious choice for me. The reason is that today everyone is familiar with and loves ETFs; it's the only way people invest. But in the early days of ETFs, everyone hated them. They were called "weapons of mass destruction," and the US Congress even held hearings on them, saying they were destroying the American Dream. People called them "unpatriotic," distrusted them, and said they would blow up the markets. But I've witnessed ETFs go from a hated technology to a mainstream way of investing, and even experienced a massive bull market. I wanted to find the next one like that. I have some good connections in the crypto space; Spencer Bogart of Blockchain Capital worked for me at ETF.com, and he introduced me to Bitwise CEO Hunter, and I've been in the crypto industry ever since. Deribit: Why are ETFs called "weapons of mass destruction"? Matt Hougan: Because they were new and posed a threat to the financial system. Does this story sound familiar? At the time, people were particularly worried about fixed-income ETFs—which put illiquid bonds into highly liquid packages—and that they would cause the market to explode. But this is actually very similar to the kind of "spreading panic" we've seen in the crypto space for years. The Financial Times said ETFs would destroy capitalism… To me, ETFs are better, faster, cheaper, and easier to trade, and the fit with the cryptocurrency model is too obvious. We're probably halfway there, but eventually cryptocurrencies will become as mainstream as ETFs are today.

    II. Bitcoin ETFs vs. Digital Asset TreasuryTreasuries

    Deribit: ETFs are clearly the entry point for traditional institutions into this space. The biggest deal last year was arguably the explosion of Digital Asset Treasurys (DATs) and everyone rushing to copy MicroStrategy's approach. What are your thoughts on the competition between ETFs and Digital Asset Treasurys? If ETFs exist, is DAT still needed?

    III. Sales Script to Institutions: Asymmetric Upside Potential

    Deribit: You've been having these conversations at the institutional level for years and have witnessed a lot of change and adoption. Now, when you go to institutions and family offices that haven't yet invested in Bitcoin or cryptocurrency, what's your sales script? Has it changed?

    Matt Hougan: Early on, you had to spend a lot of time explaining what the technology was and how it worked. We'd go to offices, take out computers, unplug Wi-Fi and speakers, and demonstrate how to create an air-gap computer and do cold storage. That was very basic 101-level education because people won't touch things they don't trust. We did that for several years.

    Now, let's delve into the main point: arguing that Bitcoin still has asymmetric upside potential, discussing how to position stablecoins and tokenization for growth, and their role in portfolios. The good thing is that Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies have shed that "alternative" label; people can view them like any other asset, and they integrate well into portfolios.

    IV. The Mathematical Logic of Bitcoin Reaching $1 Million—Digital Gold

    Deribit: Tell us more about asymmetric returns—why do you still believe?

    Matt Hougan: Absolutely! Let me calculate a conservative case for Bitcoin, conservatively over $1 million per coin. If you consider Bitcoin as digital gold, only two factors matter: the size of the store of value market and Bitcoin's share. Currently, the store of value market is $40 trillion, and Bitcoin accounts for about 4%, which is why Bitcoin is currently around $75,000.

    The mistake people make is saying, "Maybe Bitcoin could reach 25% of the market, about a quarter the size of gold, that would be a $10 trillion market capitalization, maybe five times what it is now." But this math is wrong because the store of value market itself has been growing dramatically over the past 20 years. When gold ETFs launched in 2004, the entire store of value market was only $2.5 trillion, a compound annual growth rate of 12.5% ​​over 20 years. If it continues to grow at that rate for another 10 years until 2035, Bitcoin only needs to reach 15% of the market to reach $1 million per coin; if it reaches 30%, that's $2 million. These staggering numbers are derived using very conservative assumptions. You just need to say that the store of value market continues to grow at the original rate, and Bitcoin will rise from about 5% to 15% in 10 years—that's almost a certainty, even with very conservative assumptions. This isn't a 10x growth per year. If you want 10x per year, I think some DeFi applications have that potential as token economics improve. But for mainstream assets like Bitcoin, even with relatively conservative assumptions, it could still increase 20 times within 10 years. Go look in the stock or bond markets, can you find anything like that? Deribit: I'll offer a different perspective—gold has risen 50-60% in the past 12 months, while Bitcoin has remained largely flat. What do you say? Matt Hougan: Bitcoin is an emerging store of value asset, and you can't expect it to perform exactly the same in any short period. Gold has a unique driver—central bank purchases. After the US seized Russian treasury assets, central banks bought large amounts of gold, driving up its price, not just retail or financial advisors. Bitcoin has its own unique driver—a four-year cycle, which is typical of the crypto cycle. But if you count from the start of the COVID-19 pandemic to today, this is the biggest inflation of our lifetimes; the dollar has lost 25% of its value, while Bitcoin has increased 15 times. That's a pretty good store of value asset, right? Gold hasn't always been rising either. Since the start of the Iran-Iraq War, Bitcoin has risen 14%, while gold has fallen 5%. Therefore, we need to look at the long term, not just the short term. Deribit: Are you concerned about the quantum threat? Many people attribute the underperformance of cryptocurrencies over the past few months to this. Matt Hougan: There are two levels of concern. One is that it's a challenging problem, but I think the Bitcoin community will eventually solve it, although there are some edge cases involving certain wallets that will be complicated and tricky. We're discussing it, investing resources, so I believe it will be solved. But it does significantly drag down returns. The reason is that when I go to those investment committees managing $50 billion—usually eight people in their forties and fifties—I have to convince everyone to allocate funds to Bitcoin. Over the past year, almost every meeting has ended with someone raising their hand and asking, "What about quantum?" I can roughly explain how quantum mechanics will develop, but the whole process will take 6 to 9 months, which significantly slows down institutional adoption. It will be resolved in the long term, but it's a short-term hindrance, as reflected in the starkly different price charts of Bitcoin and Bitcoin Cash over the past year. I do believe quantum mechanics has an impact, but we will get through this.

    VI. AI and Cryptocurrencies: Deflation, Liquidity, and Synergies

    Deribit: Looking at the price movements over the past few months, how do you see the relationship between AI and cryptocurrencies—complementary or competitive?

    Matt Hougan: I think they are very complementary in the long term. Beyond Bitcoin, the benefits of AI for stablecoins, tokenization, and related fields are enormous. I also believe that if you believe in the theory of AI-driven deflation—that AI triggers massive deflation, and governments respond by printing large amounts of money, just like China responded to the last major deflation when it joined the WTO—this is also positive for Bitcoin.

    Regarding the recent correlation with software stocks, I tend to think it's just a statistical anomaly, but I'm not sure. It could be due to two specific events—the four-year cycle and the deleveraging on October 10th—combined with panic selling in software stocks; the recent correlation is just a short-term anomaly. In the long run, it's a positive catalyst. Deribit: Regarding the correlation between Bitcoin and the Nasdaq, this correlation has been declining over the past six months… Matt Hougan: Yes, low correlation is one of the main reasons institutions allocate to cryptocurrencies. BlackRock's marketing materials start by talking about low correlation. Even if there was some correlation with stocks before, the 90-day or 180-day correlation is still relatively low. With this divergence, it's beneficial for cryptocurrencies. In the long run, the fundamentals driving Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies are different from most other assets. Sometimes macro trends can cause all correlations to converge to 1, but I think this different paradigm of returns is the long-term norm and is very helpful for institutional recommendations.

    VII. Cyclical Bottoming Out: Are We in a Crypto Retail Winter?

    Deribit: What's your take on Bitcoin's recent outperformance of US stocks? Is it due to capital flight from the Middle East?

    Matt Hougan: I never believe these kinds of movements have only a single cause. Capital flight from the Middle East is a major factor. Crypto sentiment has also hit all-time lows, with the Fear & Greed Index at only 4, which is a very asymmetrical reading. It's only going in the direction of normalization, and we're seeing that normalization.

    I personally have a theory that the crypto winter actually started in early 2025, not in October. So we're actually already deep into the winter, and a natural cyclical bottom is happening.

    Moreover, the world's frenzy makes cryptocurrencies appear more rational, which is beneficial for crypto. Looking at the gold/Bitcoin ratio, that bear market also started in early 2025, so perhaps we are bottoming out. Bitcoin has caught the two extreme tails of risk distribution: the far-right tail, where liquidity is abundant and everything is rising, sees Bitcoin rise the most; the far-left tail, where existing political and economic structures are challenged, and Bitcoin's characteristics as a decentralized, non-sovereign asset begin to stand out. In the middle ground, it hasn't shown many characteristics. Perhaps we are seeing Bitcoin's function as a hedging tool for the far-left tail return. Yes, I think both are true. Regarding the theory that the crypto winter started in January—the 2025 crypto market actually has two markets: Bitcoin and Ethereum performed relatively well due to large ETF inflows, while everything else fell steadily from January, with some Layer 1 tokens falling by 70% in 2025. Beyond Bitcoin and Ethereum, long-term holders and whales continued to sell throughout the year. So institutional crypto will still be rising in 2025, but crypto retail will start to collapse in early 2025. I think we're already deep in a crypto retail winter, and now we might be seeing spring. This cyclical factor is important and may be masked by ETF flows. Deribit: Has AI trading stolen attention from the crypto industry? Matt Hougan: Absolutely. AI trading and metals trading have become hot topics. Specifically in AI, miners are selling Bitcoin to invest in AI and high-performance computing centers, creating a continuous negative drag on the market. VIII. The Future of Altcoin ETFs and Institutional Flows Deribit: Will other altcoins eventually have ETFs? Matt Hougan: Technically, ETFs need futures contracts on regulated exchanges to launch in the US. I think it will eventually happen, but I don't think that will be the biggest driver. The next wave of crypto ETF growth will be thematic and index-based products—people want to bet on themes like DeFi tokens and stablecoin-related assets, not individual token ETFs, because many DeFi projects only have a market cap of a billion dollars, which is too small for the ETF market. Deribit: Will retail investors return? Or will institutions dominate the market? Matt Hougan: Institutional funds will continue to flow in over the next 10 years. Currently, the average institutional holding is 0%, which is a mistake. I think it will rise to 5%. I'm meeting with Morgan Stanley, Wells Fargo, and Merrill Lynch this week alone. This money will make the bull market more stable, less volatile, and longer-lasting. Retail funds will also return as prices rise; I don't think it will flow into AI forever. AI and crypto can be enjoyed simultaneously; these are two of the biggest market opportunities in financial history—intelligence and currency—and we can have both.

    Nine,Survival of the Fittest—Most Crypto Assets Will Go to Zero / Stablecoins / Ethereum vs.Solana

    Deribit: Many projects won't recover, they don't offer utility, they don't have network growth, and they'll slowly disappear. Then higher-quality networks will emerge, attracting more capital… Do you agree?

    Matt Hougan: I strongly agree. I think most crypto assets will go to zero, even 80% of crypto assets will go to zero, and the market will form an oligopoly, with the biggest names holding the largest share. For investors, this means not spending a lot of time trying to pick the only winner, but rather focusing on weeding out the losers—screening out projects that aren't expanding, aren't being adopted, and aren't growing, and then buying everything else.

    Focusing alpha-seeking efforts on negative screening approaches the optimal portfolio. Stablecoins are one of the biggest successes in this space to date. In 5 to 10 years, stablecoins could dominate global payments. Ethereum has benefited greatly from this, but why is Ethereum underperforming? The core question is simple: Is L1 block space a commodity? If it's a pure commodity, where investors don't care whether it's Ethereum, Solana, or another L1 or L2, and transactions flow seamlessly, then Ethereum struggles to justify its value at its current market capitalization. But if block space isn't a pure commodity, if differentiated pricing is possible, if there's a brand moat, and if cross-chain functionality isn't as easy as it seems, then Ethereum is severely undervalued—because based on its current market share, the scale of stablecoin and tokenized transaction activity is enormous, undervalued by approximately 10,000 times, especially with AI agents entering the space, where transaction volume could be astronomical. Deribit: Tradefi players are all looking at Ethereum when entering this space because it's the most trusted, the most active, and has a first-mover advantage… Matt Hougan: Absolutely agree. That's what you see in the market: Ethereum has won a lot, Solana has won a lot, and others are competing on the fringes. On Wall Street, building on Ethereum won't get you fired; building on L1 might. So Ethereum and Solana are both gaining a larger and larger share. I'm bullish on both, and I ultimately don't think L1 block space will be purely commoditized; they have a huge brand moat value. Deribit: If we entered a crypto winter early last year, do you think we're emerging from it now? Will year-end targets be significantly higher than they are now? Matt Hougan: Yes, I absolutely think so. I'm not sure if we'll hit a new all-time high this year, but it will be much better by the end of the year. There are some very positive long-term forces in crypto right now: stablecoins are a torrent, tokenization is a torrent, concerns about currency devaluation are a torrent, token economics are improving, L1 decentralization is improving, and blockchain is executing its technology roadmap better than I've seen in my eight years in crypto. These are all major trends, and overall, it's a very positive environment. Of course, there are risks: regulatory bills failing, a global recession, or other unforeseen problems. But barring those, I think it will go up significantly by the end of the year, and 2027 will be a pretty good year for crypto. XI. The Clarity Act and its Impact Deribit: How important is the Clarity Act? Matt Hougan: I think this will be a big trigger. The reason is that we are now building a regulatory foundation on sand. The current regulatory environment in the US is very good, but we are only one step away from a strong change of ruling party, which could bring a completely different regulatory environment because it is not a legislative guarantee. It will not go back to the Gensler era, but it may be worse than it is now. This risk is preventing Wall Street from investing more in this space. They are doing a lot now—mergers and acquisitions, building, hiring—but if there were a solid regulatory framework, it would be 2 to 4 times the size it is now. If the bill passes, the rest of the year will be smooth sailing for crypto; if it doesn't, we may retest the lows. Deribit: Circle has doubled in the past month. Is this pricing in a high probability of the Clarity Act passing? Matt Hougan: Yes, Circle has performed exceptionally well. I think it's pricing in the optimism surrounding the Clarity Act, and also in the expectation that it might gain a larger share of the next trillion stablecoins than the existing market. Even at its current price, I think it's still a very interesting stock. Deribit: What are the chances of the Clarity Act passing? Matt Hougan: We paid consultants in Washington, and they told us it's 50/50—I could get that conclusion myself by flipping a coin, I don't know why we paid them. That's just how it is, it's really hard to say. The window is narrowing, and Polymarket's odds are swinging between 65% and 40%. If I had to bet, I'd slightly lean towards it not passing. Washington is very distracted, and it's a complex issue.

    Thirteen,Partisanization of Crypto

    Deribit: Has Bitcoin being seen as a "Trump concept asset" become a negative factor?

    Matt Hougan: You're right, the partisanization of crypto is a risk that could trigger a counter-movement. But I don't think we'll ever go back to the Gensler era—when BlackRock, JPMorgan Chase, Croup, and others were building and investing heavily in this space, that was a huge lobbying force. They won't make stablecoins illegal or completely ban DeFi when Apollo is investing in Morpho tokens. Washington is more realistic than that. But partisanization is indeed a risk, which is why the Clarity Act is so important—it can turn what we called a sandy foundation into concrete, making it difficult to rescind even if politics changes.

data.title: Trump's "last-minute change of heart": Window of easing tensions or an escalation trap?
data.description:
Authors: Gao Shan, Xi Yue, Xinhua News Agency reporters

US President Trump stated on the 23rd that the US and Iran had held "very good and productive" talks over the past two days and announced a postponement of military strikes against Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure. Iran immediately denied any contact with the US.

Analysts believe that Trump's unilateral announcement of "US-Iran contact" and "postponement of action" reflects the multiple pressures facing the US government. Its purpose is, on the one hand, to stabilize oil prices and appease the market, and on the other hand, it may be to buy time to prepare for further escalation of the conflict. The future of the US-Israel-Iran conflict remains highly uncertain.

Have there been talks? US and Iran's statements are vastly different

On the 21st, Trump issued an "ultimatum" to Iran, demanding that Iran open the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours, or he would destroy "various power plants" in Iran. Iran responded that if the US took action, all energy and oil infrastructure in the Middle East would be considered legitimate targets and irreversibly destroyed.

Before the expiration of the US ultimatum, Trump announced on the 23rd that the US would "delay" the strike on Iranian power plants by five days, and claimed that the US-Iran dialogue was "perfect" and that key points of an agreement had been reached. Multiple US and Israeli media outlets reported that the US was in talks with Iranian Islamic Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf, and several coordinating countries were attempting to arrange a meeting between Ghalibaf, US Presidential Envoy Witkov, Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner, and US Vice President Vance later this week in Islamabad, Pakistan. However, Iran immediately denied any contact with the US. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagaei said on the 23rd that Iran had not held any negotiations with the US, and that it had received messages from some friendly countries in the past few days regarding the US demand to end the war through negotiations, and had responded appropriately based on its principled position. Ghalibaf himself also denied any negotiations with the US. An article in The New York Times points out that since the outbreak of the US-Israel-Iran war, the conflicting parties have frequently issued contradictory statements to serve their respective agendas. Other US media outlets have stated that due to a lack of clear objectives in the military action against Iran, Trump often makes contradictory statements regarding subsequent actions, sometimes even within a single day. Media and analysts believe that the Trump administration's current softening stance on Iran is mainly due to multiple pressures from both inside and outside the country. First, the ongoing conflict has driven up global oil prices, disrupted financial markets, and exacerbated inflationary pressures in the United States. As of March 23, the average retail price of gasoline in the US was close to $4 per gallon, an increase of more than $1 compared to February 28. The Washington Post reported that the Middle East conflict will cost the US economy, and the Trump administration faces a "vulnerable moment." Some analysts point out that reassuring the market is the core reason for Trump's "change of heart," and this is also a key motivation for Trump's announcement before the New York stock market opened on Monday that the US-Iran contact and strike would be postponed. Relatedly, if the war continues to impact the economy and people's livelihoods, the midterm elections will be affected. On the issue of military action against Iraq, a rift has already appeared between Trump and his voter base, the "Make America Great Again (MAGA)" camp. The Heritage Foundation, a conservative think tank, points out that if the war continues to escalate, the Democratic Party will "take control of Congress" in the midterm elections. Furthermore, America's allies are also "working hard." CNN reported on the 23rd, citing multiple sources, that America's Gulf allies privately warned Trump that the US bombing of Iranian power plants could lead to a "catastrophic escalation," and the Trump administration shifted its stance after weighing the options.

Is there another motive? A delaying tactic or a way to de-escalate?

Various interpretations suggest that the Trump administration's "last-minute change of heart" may have ulterior motives. Some media reports say that Iran believes Trump's remarks are an attempt to "lower energy prices and buy time for his military plans."

Ross Harrison, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, a US think tank, believes that this could be "a way out" to de-escalate or even end the conflict, but "it could also be a trap," namely, as Iran says, Trump buying time to prepare for expanded military action.

Liang Yabin, a professor at the Institute of International Strategic Studies of the Central Party School, analyzed that Trump's move might be a "delaying tactic."

On the one hand, after more than 20 days of airstrikes, the US military may be facing a missile shortage, making it difficult to support large-scale airstrikes and air defense missions. Replenishing and transporting ammunition will take time. On the other hand, the Trump administration may be waiting for reinforcements to be deployed, preparing for potential ground military operations. The Wall Street Journal reported on the 23rd that the US 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit will arrive in the Middle East on the 27th, which happens to be the new deadline set by Trump for Iran to "open" the Strait of Hormuz. Furthermore, Iran believes that the Trump administration's move is intended to create chaos within Iran. Iran's Fars News Agency reported on the 23rd, citing sources, that the US fabricated the false news of negotiations with Ghalibaf to discredit him, incite division within Iran, and stir up public sentiment, while simultaneously creating conditions for his assassination. Ding Long, a professor at the Middle East Institute of Shanghai International Studies University, stated that Iran is currently not at a disadvantage in the conflict, and while its willingness to negotiate is weak, it also faces real difficulties such as escalating arms depletion and economic and livelihood hardship. Conversely, the US stance has softened somewhat, and the situation may see a scenario of fighting while negotiating, or using the conflict to promote dialogue. Ding Long also pointed out that the future of the US-Israel-Iran war remains uncertain, and it cannot be ruled out that the US is feigning détente while actually preparing for war. However, the willingness of countries like Pakistan to mediate is a positive sign, and this conflict can ultimately only be truly ended through diplomatic negotiations.

data.title: OpenAI Closes Sora After Viral Launch, Cancels $1 Billion Disney Character Licensing Deal
data.description:
OpenAI Shuts Down Sora After Plans With Disney Collapse

OpenAI has confirmed it is closing Sora, its AI video-generation platform that allowed users to create short videos from text prompts.

The announcement came in a social media post on Tuesday, stating:

“We’re saying goodbye to Sora. To everyone who created with Sora, shared it, and built community around it: thank you. What you made with Sora mattered, and we know this news is disappointing.”

We’re saying goodbye to the Sora app. To everyone who created with Sora, shared it, and built community around it: thank you. What you made with Sora mattered, and we know this news is disappointing.

We’ll share more soon, including timelines for the app and API and details on…

— Sora (@soraofficialapp) March 24, 2026

Why Did OpenAI End Sora

The company explained that the decision aligns with a shift in focus towards “world simulation research to advance robotics” and its broader roadmap to artificial general intelligence.

OpenAI stated that the closure applies to Sora as a consumer app and API, while existing AI image-generation features within ChatGPT will remain unaffected.

Disney Partnership Collapses Amid Shutdown

A major factor in Sora’s closure was the collapse of a proposed $1 billion investment from Disney, which had been tied to a three-year agreement allowing more than 200 of its characters to be used in AI-generated videos.

Disney has cancelled their deal with OpenAI.

They planned to invest $1 billion into the company and let Sora have access to AI generate videos of characters from Disney, Marvel, Pixar and Star Wars.

(Source: https://t.co/9q8u5VtqZE) pic.twitter.com/tJXdArKz9X

— DiscussingFilm (@DiscussingFilm) March 24, 2026

Sources told online reports that the investment never materialised, and no money changed hands.

The shutdown came just 30 minutes after a collaborative meeting with Disney teams, leaving them blindsided.

A Disney spokesperson confirmed that the company respects OpenAI’s decision to exit the video-generation business and will continue exploring other ways to engage with AI responsibly.

Sora’s Rise and Rapid Fall

Launched in February 2024, Sora quickly drew attention as a text-to-video model capable of turning written prompts into short clips.

The platform expanded with Sora 2 and a standalone mobile app, introducing social features like a video feed and “cameos,” which allowed users to insert themselves into AI-generated scenes.

Despite its initial popularity, the platform faced criticism over copyright and ethical concerns.

Experts warned that Sora could recreate recognisable characters, produce misleading news footage, and spread misinformation.

OpenAI's Sora system card all but confirms it is trained on copyrighted videos without a license: it uses "publicly available data" from "web crawls".

This is what companies say when they train models on copyrighted work without a license.https://t.co/IcSe6SA6l5pic.twitter.com/09tuswd7cg

— Ed Newton-Rex (@ednewtonrex) December 9, 2024

Legal pressures forced the removal of copyrighted characters from user-generated content, which reduced engagement.

OpenAI has quietly started tightening the guardrails in Sora.

You can’t generate videos of celebrities like Michael Jackson anymore, looks like the managers and estates of those stars have officially opted out.

The wild west era of AI-generated celebrity clips might be ending… pic.twitter.com/TXSbg8rBB3

— VraserX e/acc (@VraserX) October 3, 2025

According to sources, running Sora was costly, reportedly around $15 million per day, straining resources that could be redirected to more profitable areas, such as enterprise tools and coding products.

OpenAI executives had debated the platform’s future internally, but some team members were reportedly surprised by the abrupt announcement, which came shortly after a meeting with Disney teams.

What Happens to Users and Content

OpenAI confirmed it will provide further details, including timelines for shutting down the app and API, and guidance on preserving content already created by users.

The company acknowledged the Sora community’s contributions, noting the impact users made by sharing and generating content on the platform.

OpenAI just shut down its most used video product while it was still growing.

The company that promised to reshape Hollywood with artificial intelligence is killing the Sora app.

Here is what makes this shocking.

Sora's daily active users had climbed from zero in October… https://t.co/SVknI48annpic.twitter.com/hY5VI8Y57h

— Milk Road AI (@MilkRoadAI) March 24, 2026

Is AI Video Generation Over for OpenAI

While Sora as a social app is ending, OpenAI is not abandoning AI video entirely.

Video-generation capabilities continue to exist within its wider ecosystem, including ChatGPT integrations.

The closure signals a pivot away from a social media-style format rather than a withdrawal from the underlying technology.

OpenAI Focuses on Robotics and AGI

Executives are now concentrating on consolidating AI capabilities into a single “super-app” and pursuing enterprise and coding products.

Fidji Simo, recently renamed CEO of AGI deployment, held staff meetings to discuss the company’s focus on higher-productivity use cases.

CEO Sam Altman also restructured leadership, with security and safety teams no longer reporting directly to him.

OpenAI just exited the video generation business entirely. App dead. API dead. No video inside ChatGPT. Disney’s $1 billion deal, signed four months ago, is dead.
